text_blocks.snippet: The Nissan Ariya sold in Japan is primarily compatible with the CHAdeMO fast charging standard. This differs from North American and European models, which use CCS (Combined Charging System).
Domestic models feature a dual-port setup to support the most common public infrastructure in Japan:

list.snippet: Fast Charging (DC): Uses the CHAdeMO connector.
Slow/Home Charging (AC): Uses the J1772 (Type 1) connector.

text_blocks.snippet: The Ariya is designed to work with Japan's extensive public charging networks, which are heavily built around the CHAdeMO standard.

list.snippet: e-Mobility Power (eMP): This is the largest network in Japan. The Ariya can utilize its vast array of chargers found at roadside stations (Michi-no-Eki), service areas, and commercial facilities.
High-Power Charging: Japanese spec vehicles support up to 130kW DC fast charging.
Next-Gen Infrastructure: Recent developments in Japan include 350kW+ chargers, which are being developed to ensure long-term compatibility with high-voltage vehicles.

Tesla Superchargers: Currently, Nissan is transitioning toward NACS (Tesla standard) in North America. In Japan, some Tesla chargers may require a CHAdeMO adapter if they do not provide a native CHAdeMO cable.

text_blocks.snippet: Watch how to properly utilize rapid charging infrastructure and manage battery health for the Nissan Ariya:

list.snippet: Provide a list of apps to find chargers in Japan
Explain the cost of charging at public stations in Japan
Help you find compatible home charging equipment for your region

serpapi_response.reconstructed_markdown:
The Nissan Ariya sold in Japan is primarily compatible with the CHAdeMO fast charging standard. This differs from North American and European models, which use [CCS](https://www.zapmap.com/ev-guides/model-charging/nissan-ariya) \(Combined Charging System\). [0] [1] [5] [3]

### Standard Connectors in Japan

Domestic models feature a dual\-port setup to support the most common public infrastructure in Japan: [8] [9]

- Fast Charging \(DC\): Uses the CHAdeMO connector.
- Slow/Home Charging \(AC\): Uses the [J1772](https://recharged.com/articles/nissan-ariya-best-home-charger) \(Type 1\) connector. [5] [10] [6]

### Network Compatibility

The Ariya is designed to work with Japan's extensive public charging networks, which are heavily built around the CHAdeMO standard. [3] [2] [11]

- e\-Mobility Power \(eMP\): This is the largest network in Japan. The Ariya can utilize its vast array of chargers found at [roadside stations](https://teskas.net/t/topic/12864?tl=en) \(Michi\-no\-Eki\), service areas, and commercial facilities.
- High\-Power Charging: Japanese spec vehicles support up to 130kW DC fast charging.
- Next\-Gen Infrastructure: Recent developments in Japan include [350kW chargers](https://www.chademo.com/japan-opens-to-1000v-charging), which are being developed to ensure long\-term compatibility with high\-voltage vehicles.
- Tesla Superchargers: Currently, Nissan is transitioning toward [NACS](https://global.nissannews.com/en/releases/nissan-to-adopt-nacs-for-ariya-and-future-ev-models) \(Tesla standard\) in North America. In Japan, some Tesla chargers may require a CHAdeMO adapter if they do not provide a native CHAdeMO cable. [2] [9] [5] [0]

### Comparison by Region

| Market | Fast Charging \(DC\) | Normal Charging \(AC\) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Japan | CHAdeMO | J1772 |
| USA / Canada | CCS1 / NACS \(2025\+\) | J1772 |
| Europe | CCS2 | Type 2 |
[0] [12] [13] [14]
